summary: I was born in Taiwan in the late 1980s, and my family has strong roots there. My parents were born in Taiwan before moving to Thailand as adults, and my brother was also born there. Although I left Taiwan at a young age, it remains an important part of my identity and family history.

After being born in Taiwan, I moved to Thailand with my family in November 1988. I spent most of my childhood there, living in Bangkok and attending an international English-speaking school. My parents had moved there as adults, and my brother was also born in Taiwan but grew up in Thailand before studying abroad.

I came to the United States in August 2005 to attend Macalester College in Saint Paul. My brother had already been studying here at Grinnell College for three years, which helped me navigate the process of applying and moving. Living in the U.S. gave me a unique sense of independence, especially because I was comfortable with English and could easily understand my surroundings, unlike my experiences in Taiwan and Thailand.

I started my undergraduate studies at Macalester College in Saint Paul in August 2005. It was there that I met my wife, Marie Wu, and began to build my life in the United States. Macalester was my first academic home in the U.S., and it played a significant role in my transition from an international student to someone considering long-term opportunities here.

In 2010, I began my graduate studies in sociology at the University of Minnesota. This was a continuation of my academic journey after Macalester College, and it allowed me to deepen my understanding of social issues. Studying at the University of Minnesota in Minneapolis has been an important chapter in my life as an immigrant and scholar.

My older brother enrolled at Grinnell College in the United States three years before I came here. His experience provided me with a template for applying to college, getting a visa, and moving to a new country. Knowing he was at Grinnell helped ease my transition when I decided to study in the U.S.
cues: helped with application and visa process

summary: I lived in Bangkok during my childhood after moving from Taiwan. It was there that I attended an international K-12 English-speaking school, which greatly influenced my language skills. Bangkok was the city where I grew up and received my early education before moving to the United States.

I learned English at an international K-12 school in Bangkok, which was crucial for my academic and social life. This training made me comfortable with English and influenced my decision to pursue higher education in an Anglophone country like the United States. Being proficient in English gave me a sense of independence and confidence when I arrived in the U.S.
